main_summary.summary: Shirley Miller Sherrod, born 1947 and raised in the Hawkins-family settlement of Baker County, Georgia, describes growing up in a Black landowning family in a county controlled by Sheriff L. Warren 'Gator' Johnson, whose speed-trap operation generated roughly $150,000 a year per Atlanta Constitution reporting. Her father, Hosea Miller, was shot by white farmer Cal Hall in March 1965 in a cattle dispute and died ten days later; a white grand jury refused to indict. The night of his death she committed to remain in the South to work for change. She entered the Albany/Southwest Georgia movement that June at age 17, met SNCC field secretary Charles Sherrod, and married him; their Albany office was firebombed in July 1967. With Slater King and others she helped found New Communities, a 6,000-acre Georgia land trust modeled on the Israeli Moshav, until Governor Lester Maddox blocked OEO funding and the property was foreclosed in 1985. She joined the Federation of Southern Cooperatives, organized Black and white farmers, and pursued the Pigford lawsuit. In July 2009 New Communities received a $12 million ruling. She and her husband used the settlement to purchase Cypress Pond Plantation outside Albany.
key_themes: Baker County Georgia and Sheriff Gator Johnson's regime
New Communities land trust and the Moshav/Kibbutz model
Federation of Southern Cooperatives and cross-racial farmer organizing
SNCC Southwest Georgia project and the Albany movement
main_summary.historical_significance:
Sherrod's testimony is a first-person account of three interlocking strands of post-1965 civil rights history that are under-documented in the standard movement narrative: the New Communities land-trust experiment as one of the earliest U.S. applications of the Israeli Moshav model to African American cooperative agriculture; the Pigford v. Glickman / Pigford II discrimination lawsuit against USDA, told from the perspective of a Track A claimant who eventually won a $12 million New Communities judgment after a 10-year fight; and the survival of the Southwest Georgia movement after SNCC's 1966-67 break over the Black Power question, when Sherrod and her husband Charles incorporated as the Southwest Georgia Project to retain white organizers.

The interview also preserves a detailed eyewitness account of pre-1965 Baker County voter-suppression history, naming Benjamin T. Coney, Ike King, Carl Broadway, and 'Uncle Josh' as the 1950s registration pioneers whose houses and cars were bombed before the formal movement reached the county, and locates the 1965 murder of Hosea Miller within that longer arc.

chapters.title: Hawkins-Family Settlement
chapters.summary: Sherrod opens with the genealogy of her family's land in Baker County, Georgia, traced through the 1870 census to her grandmother's Hawkins family. Her grandmother and grandfather Julius Miller received 31 acres and accumulated enough land to give each of their 14 children a minimum 31-acre share, forming the Hawkins-family settlement.

chapters.title: Gator Johnson's Baker County
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes Sheriff L. Warren 'Gator' Johnson's control of Baker County, including a speed-trap operation that the Atlanta Constitution later reported generated about $150,000 a year. She contrasts the Black-owned land of the Hawkins settlement with the major plantations in the county, including Ichauway Plantation owned by Robert Woodruff of Coca-Cola and Pineland Plantation owned by the Mellon family.

chapters.title: Segregated Schools and Cucumbers
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes attending school in army barracks through fourth grade and then in Methodist churches while a new segregated school was built, moving into that building in April 1957. She also recounts the cucumber-picking work that distinguished children from Black-owned land from those on the plantations, and the everyday humiliations of hand-me-down textbooks and bus breakdowns.

chapters.title: Pre-Movement Voter Registration
chapters.summary: Sherrod recounts the 1950s Baker County voter-registration efforts led by her uncle 'Uncle Josh,' Benjamin T. Coney, Ike King, and Carl Broadway, whose houses and cars were bombed and on whose property moonshine was planted. She describes her parents' own attempt to register, and the Farmers Home Administration's refusal to lend her father money to build a brick home because, he was told, a Black man could not borrow to build in brick.

chapters.title: The Murder of Hosea Miller
chapters.summary: On March 14, 1965, Sherrod's father Hosea Miller was shot by white farmer Cal Hall during a dispute over cattle that had wandered onto Miller property. He died on March 25, ten days after the shooting. A white grand jury refused to indict Cal Hall. In a later civil suit, attorney C.B. King attempted to seat jurors who would acknowledge that Black people tell the truth; the jury still returned for the defendant.

chapters.title: Commitment to the South
chapters.summary: The night of her father's death, looking out at the moon, Sherrod made the private decision to abandon her plan to leave Georgia for the North and to remain in the South to work for change. She describes her first mass meeting, where she saw Black tenants from Ichauway Plantation and Pineland Plantation publicly committing to fight despite the risk. At 17 she joined the Baker County movement that began in June 1965.

chapters.title: Caravan to Washington
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes secretly organizing a five-car caravan from Thankful Baptist Church to Washington to testify before members of Congress and march at the Justice Department about injustices in Baker County. The caravan routed east through Mitchell County, Georgia to avoid Gator Johnson's expected ambush. The trip also produced an injunction against Johnson that constrained his speed-trap operation.

chapters.title: SNCC Break and the Southwest Georgia Project
chapters.summary: Sherrod transferred from Fort Valley State to Albany State in 1967. She describes the 1966-67 SNCC transition from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael and the Black Power directive that white organizers should leave. The Southwest Georgia organizers disagreed and incorporated independently as the Southwest Georgia Project to retain the white workers already in the field.

chapters.title: Marriage and Albany Office Firebombing
chapters.summary: Sherrod married Charles Sherrod in 1966. In the summer of 1967, white men set their Albany house and SNCC newsletter office on fire while she was inside; the neighborhood child Grady Christian was on the property and walked the attackers around to identify her husband. The Sherrods left for Baker County that night, had a flat tire en route, and were unexpectedly helped by two white men who did not recognize Charles.

chapters.title: Mother Elected, Gator Retires
chapters.summary: Eleven years after Hosea Miller's murder, Grace Hollis Miller ran for and won election as the first Black official in Baker County. The same election cycle saw Gator Johnson retire and his son 'Scrut' Johnson succeed him as sheriff. Sherrod describes a courthouse standoff on election night between Gator Johnson and Charles Sherrod, broken when Scrut Johnson came in and pulled his father away.
keywords: first Black elected official Baker County

chapters.title: Founding New Communities
chapters.summary: After tenants were evicted from white-owned land for movement participation, seven Southwest Georgia organizers traveled to Israel to study the Kibbutz model. Sherrod explains the group ultimately adopted the Moshav rather than the Kibbutz, because the Kibbutz required children to live apart from parents. New Communities incorporated as a land trust on a 6,000-acre plot north of Albany, with Slater King arranging year-one financing through Prudential Insurance after Governor Lester Maddox blocked OEO funding.

chapters.title: Innovative Farm Operations
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes the New Communities farm as a cooperative enterprise organized through a farm committee that included all farm workers plus three board members and the farm manager. The operation included a 75-head brood-sow operation with on-farm processing of cured meats, sugar-cane syrup pressed on-site, an old-fashioned smokehouse on U.S. Highway 19, muscadine grape production in the 1970s before others in the region attempted it, and a community-run land trust where neither Sherrod nor her husband occupied the largest house.

chapters.title: Federation of Southern Cooperatives
chapters.summary: After the 1985 foreclosure of New Communities, Sherrod entered the Rural Development Leadership Network institute at UC Davis and was sponsored by the Federation of Southern Cooperatives, which then hired her in July 1985. The land was sold at auction in September 1985; the new owner razed the houses and the Highway 19 farmers market. Sherrod organized Black farmers across the South for the Federation and pursued an MBA at Valdosta State through Albany State extension classes.

chapters.title: Origins of Pigford
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes the meeting at which the Federation, the Land Loss Prevention Project in North Carolina, and the Farmers Legal Action Group in Minnesota decided to pursue a discrimination lawsuit against USDA. After a Texas attorney intercepted the original six test cases (he was later disbarred), the Pigford case surfaced. The plaintiffs settled rather than going to trial because most farmers lacked complete documentation; Track A claimants needed minimal proof for a $50,000 payout and debt-write-off, while Track B claimants pursued individualized hearings.

chapters.title: New Communities Pigford Victory
chapters.summary: Driving back from Eufaula, Alabama, Sherrod realized New Communities was still farming in 1981 and could itself file a Pigford claim. Reviewing the Lee County courthouse records, the Sherrods documented that USDA had lent money to white plantation owners while denying their applications. The first 2002 hearing was lost when the hearing officer (the wife of the Pigford chief adjudicator) ruled against them. After a four-year monitor's-office review, the chief adjudicator ruled in July 2009 that New Communities would receive approximately $12 million.

chapters.title: Cypress Pond Plantation
chapters.summary: With the Pigford proceeds, the Sherrods purchased Cypress Pond Plantation outside Albany, including an 1851 antebellum home, an 85-acre lake, cabins, and farming land. The property had been listed at $21 million and reduced to $6.9 million; the Sherrods negotiated to $4.5 million with equipment. Sherrod notes that the original owner had been the wealthiest slaveholder in Georgia, holding approximately 1,000 enslaved people across three properties; Jefferson Davis is said to have stayed at the property during Confederate succession planning.

chapters.title: Race, Class, and the Spooners
chapters.summary: Sherrod reflects on her work helping white farmers after her experience helping Black farmers had become known. She names the Spooners as the first white family she helped save their farm in the 1980s, the same episode that would later surface in a controversial 2010 video clip. She frames the lesson as one about poverty rather than race, and on the gendered dynamics of being a woman organizing male farmers, including a Heifer Project hog-cooperative training incident in Harris County, Georgia.

chapters.title: Ben and Jerry's Pecan Partnership
chapters.summary: Sherrod closes with the story of organizing Black pecan farmers to supply Ben and Jerry's ice cream after Rapunzel Page proposed at a farm conference that the company would buy product to address Black land loss. White processors offered tokenistic transactions; Ben and Jerry's brought in its own supplier, paid market-plus-premium, and Sherrod organized farmers to save half the premium toward a processing facility, which they acquired in 1997 and which is still operating, women-operated.
related_events: Ben and Jerry's pecan supply contract
